text: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have been extensively investigated. The underlying knowledge of the dynamic behavior of SACs during catalysis is unfortunately lacking. Here, the evolution of active sites over a Pd/TiO2-anatase SAC, denoted as Pd1/TiO2 (Pd1: single Pd atom) during reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) is investigated. The influence of kinetic parameters and in situ observations along with theoretical calculations reveal that at temperatures (T) above 350 °C, the rWGS over Pd1/TiO2 is catalyzed by hydrogen (H2) activated Pd sites with partially cleaved Pd-O interfacial bonds that exhibit unique electronic characteristics resulting in high intrinsic rWGS activity via carboxyl pathway. The observation of activation of Pd1 sites by H2 and subsequent partial sintering into disordered flat clusters of diameter around 1 nm (denoted as Pdn) is reported. These highly active Pd sites with the new coordination environment are readily inactivated by oxidation. When oxidation is carried out at higher temperatures, Pdn clusters are redispersed and the TiO2 is fully reduced. In CO, Pd1 sites sinter into large crystalline particles of diameter around 5 nm (denoted as PdNP). During rWGS over Pd1/TiO2, the two pathways of Pd evolution are observed, where the activation of Pd1 sites by H2 dominates and is responsible for the increasing rate with time-on-stream and the presence of steady state Pd active sites that are similar to those formed under H2. This study for the first time demonstrates the coordination environment and nuclearity of the metal sites on the surface of a SAC can evolve under different catalytic conditions and pretreatments and how these evolutions modulate the activity of the resulting metal sites. This work not only sheds light on the dynamic behavior of SACs during catalysis, but also provides fundamental understanding of the structure-function relationship over SACs.
